title: Tachycardia With a Pulse
learningObjectives: Differentiate stable from unstable tachycardia using clinical signs of hemodynamic compromise.
State when immediate synchronized cardioversion is indicated for unstable tachycardia.
Outline the management pathway for stable regular narrow-complex tachycardia (SVT).
Describe the adenosine dosing protocol (6 mg, then 12 mg) including administration technique.
Distinguish between stable and unstable wide-complex tachycardia and the rationale for assuming VT when uncertain.
State the dose and administration of amiodarone for stable wide-complex tachycardia.
Describe the management of irregular narrow-complex tachycardia (atrial fibrillation/flutter) for rate control.
Explain why polymorphic VT is treated with unsynchronized defibrillation rather than synchronized cardioversion.
Identify procainamide as an alternative to amiodarone for stable wide-complex tachycardia.

summary.title: Stable vs Unstable Tachycardia — The First Decision
summary.content: The ACLS approach to tachycardia with a pulse begins with a single critical assessment: is the patient stable or unstable? Unstable tachycardia shows signs of hemodynamic compromise — hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg), acutely altered mental status, chest pain, acute heart failure, or signs of shock. An unstable patient with tachycardia requires immediate synchronized cardioversion regardless of the rhythm type. A stable patient allows time for diagnosis and pharmacologic or vagal therapy.

summary.title: Immediate Synchronized Cardioversion — When and How
summary.content: Unstable tachycardia = immediate synchronized cardioversion. Energy levels: regular narrow-complex SVT starts at 100 J biphasic; atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter start at ≥200 J biphasic; regular monomorphic VT starts at 100 J biphasic. Sedate if the patient is conscious and time permits. Polymorphic VT, however, is treated with UNSYNCHRONIZED defibrillation (not synchronized cardioversion), because the irregular waveform makes reliable R-wave synchronization impossible and delays are dangerous.

summary.title: Stable Narrow-Complex Regular Tachycardia — SVT
summary.content: Most regular narrow-complex tachycardias are supraventricular in origin (AV nodal reentrant tachycardia, AV reentrant tachycardia). For stable patients, start with vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid sinus massage). If ineffective, give adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push via a proximal (near-the-heart) IV port followed by a rapid 20 mL saline flush; if no response, give 12 mg and may repeat once. Adenosine works by transiently blocking AV nodal conduction, terminating reentrant circuits that depend on the AV node.

summary.title: Stable Narrow-Complex Irregular Tachycardia
summary.content: Irregular narrow-complex tachycardia is most commonly atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter with variable block. For stable patients, rate control is the priority: beta-blockers (metoprolol), calcium-channel blockers (diltiazem), or amiodarone if both rate control and rhythm control are considered. Target a resting heart rate <110/min (lenient) or <80/min (strict) per clinical context. Adenosine is not effective for AF/AFlutter rate control but can be diagnostic to unmask flutter waves.

summary.title: Stable Wide-Complex Regular Tachycardia — VT vs SVT with Aberrancy
summary.content: A regular wide-complex tachycardia should be assumed to be ventricular tachycardia (VT) until proven otherwise — this is the safest assumption, as treating SVT as VT is less harmful than treating VT as SVT. Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes is first-line for stable wide-complex tachycardia, and may be repeated once. Procainamide 20–50 mg/min IV is a reasonable alternative. If the patient becomes unstable at any point, switch to synchronized cardioversion starting at 100 J.

summary.title: Polymorphic VT — A Special Case
summary.content: Polymorphic VT (including torsades de pointes) is a wide-complex tachycardia with a continuously varying QRS axis and morphology. Unlike monomorphic VT, polymorphic VT is treated with UNSYNCHRONIZED defibrillation — synchronization is unreliable on an irregular waveform and delays defibrillation. Magnesium sulfate 1–2 g IV is given specifically for torsades de pointes. Correct underlying electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) promptly.

summary.title: Procainamide as an Alternative Antiarrhythmic
summary.content: Procainamide 20–50 mg/min IV (maximum cumulative dose 17 mg/kg) is an alternative to amiodarone for stable wide-complex tachycardia. It is effective for both regular and irregular wide-complex tachycardias. Monitor for hypotension (a common side effect) and QT prolongation. Procainamide is not recommended in patients with structural heart disease or significant heart failure, where amiodarone is preferred.

clinicalPearls: Unstable tachycardia = immediate synchronized cardioversion — do not waste time on drug therapy.
When in doubt, assume VT — treating SVT as VT is safer than the reverse.
Adenosine must be given as a rapid IV push through a proximal (near-the-heart) IV port, followed by a rapid saline flush, to reach the heart before it is metabolized.
Polymorphic VT = UNSYNCHRONIZED defibrillation — synchronization is contraindicated on an irregular waveform.
Procainamide causes hypotension — give slowly and monitor blood pressure; it is a poor choice in cardiogenic shock.
redFlags: Attempting synchronized cardioversion on polymorphic VT (synchronization is contraindicated).
Giving adenosine through a distal IV port (too slow — drug is metabolized before reaching the heart).
Assuming a wide-complex tachycardia is SVT with aberrancy and giving verapamil (may cause hemodynamic collapse if it is VT).
Delayed cardioversion in an unstable tachycardic patient while attempting drug therapy.
Not recognizing atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response as the cause of hemodynamic instability.
commonMistakes: Hesitating to cardiovert an unstable patient because 'we should try drugs first.'
Using adenosine for rate control in atrial fibrillation — adenosine terminates AV-nodal-dependent reentrant tachycardias, not fibrillation.
Forgetting to set the defib to SYNCHRONIZED mode for cardioversion (risk of R-on-T precipitation of VF).
Giving procainamide too rapidly, causing hypotension in an already unstable patient.
Not confirming a regular vs irregular rhythm before choosing adenosine over rate-control agents.
memoryAids: Wide-complex tachycardia = VT until proven otherwise.
Adenosine 6, then 12 — rapid push, proximal port, fast flush.
Poly VT = UNSYNC defibrillation — you can't sync what isn't regular.
Amiodarone 150 over 10 for wide-complex; procainamide is the alternative, but watch the pressure.

decisionTrees.title: Tachycardia With a Pulse — ACLS Decision Algorithm
decisionTrees.content:
Tachycardia identified (rate >100/min with a pulse)
  → Assess for instability: hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, acute heart failure, shock
     → UNSTABLE: Immediate synchronized cardioversion
        → Sedate if conscious and time permits
        → Energy: 100 J for regular narrow or regular monomorphic VT; ≥200 J for AF/AFlutter
        → If polymorphic VT: UNSYNCHRONIZED defibrillation (not synchronized)
     → STABLE: Assess rhythm
        → Narrow-complex, REGULAR (SVT):
           → Vagal maneuvers first
           → If ineffective: Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push (proximal port + flush)
           → If no response: 12 mg adenosine (may repeat once)
           → If still refractory: beta-blocker or calcium-channel blocker
        → Narrow-complex, IRREGULAR (AF/AFlutter):
           → Rate control: diltiazem, metoprolol, or amiodarone
           → Target resting HR <110/min (lenient) or <80/min (strict)
           → Consider anticoagulation per guidelines
        → Wide-complex, REGULAR (assume VT):
           → Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min (may repeat once)
           → Alternative: Procainamide 20–50 mg/min IV
           → If becomes unstable → synchronized cardioversion at 100 J
        → Wide-complex, IRREGULAR:
           → If polymorphic VT → UNSYNCHRONIZED defibrillation + magnesium
           → If pre-excited AF → avoid AV-nodal blockers; consider procainamide or cardioversion
  → In parallel: seek and treat reversible causes (hypoxia, electrolytes, toxins, ischemia)

references: AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 9: Adult Advanced Life Support (Tachycardia With a Pulse)
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Executive Summary (Synchronized cardioversion energies, adenosine protocol)
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 9 (Procainamide, polymorphic VT management)
